children.name: Ciconia
children.description: Large soaring birds of open farmland and wetlands. The White Stork nested on Swedish farmsteads for centuries before disappearing in the 20th century due to drainage of wet meadows; an active reintroduction programme centred on Skåne has re-established a breeding population. The Black Stork is a shy forest bird, a rare but increasing breeder.

children.name: Ciconia ciconia
children.description: The white stork once nested on chimneys across southern Sweden before vanishing with the draining of wet meadows; a Skåne reintroduction project has restored a small breeding population. It feeds on frogs, insects, and small mammals in open grassland.

children.name: Ciconia nigra
children.description: A shy, glossy-black forest stork that nests in tall trees near undisturbed wetlands, in contrast to its conspicuous white relative. A rare but slowly increasing breeder and migrant in Sweden.

children.name: Ciconia abdimii
children.description: Named after Ibrahim Bey Abdim, Turkish governor of Wadi Halfa in Sudan when the type specimen was collected. A small black-and-white stork that breeds in the Sahel belt and East Africa, migrating south into southern Africa after the rains. Locally welcomed as an omen of good fortune; congregates in flocks of thousands on grasslands to feed on locusts and insects.

children.name: Ciconia episcopus
children.description: Named for its contrasting black 'bishop's cap' and white woolly neck, this widespread stork occurs patchily across tropical Asia and Africa. Found in wetlands, cultivated fields, and open woodland; often seen singly or in pairs. Feeds on frogs, fish, and large invertebrates in shallow water.

children.name: Ciconia boyciana
children.description: One of the world's most endangered storks, with fewer than 3,000 individuals remaining. Breeds along rivers and wetlands in the Russian Far East and northeastern China; winters in the Yangtze River basin. Slightly larger than the White Stork, with a distinctive black bill; a flagship species for wetland conservation in East Asia. IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Ciconia stormi
children.description: A critically endangered stork restricted to lowland riparian forest and rivers in Borneo, Sumatra, Peninsular Malaysia, and southern Thailand. One of the world's least-known large wading birds; its strict dependence on intact lowland forest makes it acutely vulnerable to logging and wetland conversion. IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Ciconia maguari
children.description: A tall, elegant stork of South American grasslands and wetlands, from Venezuela south to Patagonia. Feeds on fish, frogs, and large invertebrates in shallow water and seasonally flooded pampa. Its distinctive red facial skin and pinkish bill base set it apart from all other storks; builds bulky platform nests in marsh vegetation. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Leptoptilos
children.description: The adjutant storks — the largest members of the family, with massive wedge-shaped bills, bald heads, and pendulous neck pouches. The Marabou of Africa is a supreme scavenger, joining vultures at carcasses and thriving near human habitation. The two Asian adjutants — the Lesser and Greater — are among the world's most endangered storks, threatened by wetland loss and hunting.

children.name: Leptoptilos crumenifer
children.description: Africa's largest flying bird by wingspan (up to 3.7 m), the Marabou is a naked-headed scavenger of savanna, wetlands, and rubbish tips. Its inflatable pink neck sac serves in thermoregulation and courtship display. Fills the ecological role of a vulture at lion kills; hugely abundant in East African towns. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Leptoptilos javanicus
children.description: A large Asian stork with a bare wrinkled head, found in mangroves, rice paddies, and freshwater wetlands from India to Java and Borneo. Unlike the Marabou it feeds almost entirely on live prey — fish, frogs, and crabs in shallow water. IUCN Vulnerable due to wetland drainage, nest-site disturbance, and hunting.

children.name: Leptoptilos dubius
children.description: Endangered, with fewer than 1,200 individuals remaining in Assam, India and Cambodia. Scavenges carcasses as well as taking live prey; historically widespread, it has lost most of its range to wetland conversion and persecution. Unlikely conservation icon: thriving urban colonies in Guwahati nest among rooftops and feed on rubbish tips. IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Anastomus
children.description: The openbills — two medium-sized storks named for the remarkable gap that develops between upper and lower mandibles in adults. This morphological feature is a precise adaptation for extracting apple snails and freshwater mussels from their shells; the bill closes only at the tip and base, creating lateral blades that slice open the snail's operculum. Openbills are highly nomadic, tracking seasonal floods that sustain their molluscan prey.

children.name: Anastomus oscitans
children.description: A white-and-black stork of South and Southeast Asian wetlands, rice paddies, and flood plains. The characteristic open-bill gap allows it to extract freshwater snails and bivalves with precision. Nests colonially in heronries, sometimes in thousands; populations have expanded with irrigated rice cultivation. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Anastomus lamelligerus
children.description: The African counterpart of the Asian openbill, sharing the same interdigitating mandible gap used for extracting apple snails and freshwater bivalves. Dark-plumaged (glossy black with a bronze or greenish gloss) and highly nomadic across sub-Saharan African wetlands, following seasonal flooding that sustains its mollusc prey. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Ephippiorhynchus
children.description: Two large, spectacular storks — the Saddle-billed Stork of Africa and the Black-necked Stork of Asia and Australia — united by their enormous, brightly coloured bills. Both are apex wading birds of tropical wetlands, relying on large territories with abundant fish. The saddle-bill's tricoloured bill is among the most elaborate in the stork family.

children.name: Ephippiorhynchus senegalensis
children.description: Africa's tallest stork, instantly recognisable by its extraordinary tricoloured bill — red, black, and yellow, with a yellow frontal 'saddle' shield at the base. Solitary and territorial along large wetlands, floodplains, and rivers across sub-Saharan Africa; catches fish and frogs with fast, jabbing strikes. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus
children.description: Australia's only stork and one of its most imposing birds, standing 1.3 m tall with iridescent black-and-white plumage and a massive black bill. Also found across South and Southeast Asia. Unique among storks: females have yellow irides while males have dark brown — an unusual form of sexual dimorphism. Nests on tall trees in floodplain habitats. IUCN Least Concern overall but declining in much of Asia.

children.name: Jabiru
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Jabiru — the tallest flying bird in the Americas. With its bare black neck, vivid red neck band, and massive upturned bill, the Jabiru is unmistakeable across the tropical lowlands from Mexico to Argentina. It is especially prominent in the Pantanal, the world's largest tropical wetland, where it is considered a flagship species for conservation.

children.name: Jabiru mycteria
children.description: The tallest flying bird in the Americas, standing up to 1.4 m with a wingspan of 2.3–2.8 m. Found in tropical lowlands from Mexico to Argentina, most abundant in the Pantanal. Its massive, slightly upturned bill catches fish, frogs, and snakes in shallow water; pairs mate for life and reuse their enormous platform nests for decades. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Mycteria
children.description: The wood storks — four medium to large storks found across the Americas, Africa, and Asia. All share a bare, often colourful head and neck and a decurved bill used in tactile, open-and-snap feeding: the bill is held slightly open while the stork sweeps it through murky water, snapping shut on contact with prey. This technique allows efficient fishing in turbid conditions where visual predation would fail. All are colonial nesters, often sharing large heronries with herons and ibises.

children.name: Mycteria americana
children.description: North America's only native stork, breeding in cypress swamps and mangroves from the southeastern United States through Central America to Argentina. Relies on tactile bill-snapping to catch fish in murky water; colonial nesting in large rookeries. Listed as Threatened in the US due to Everglades drainage reducing prey concentrations. IUCN Least Concern globally.

children.name: Mycteria cinerea
children.description: A white-and-black coastal stork of mangroves and tidal mudflats from Myanmar to the Philippines and the Indonesian archipelago. The most marine-associated Mycteria stork; forages in mangrove channels and tidal flats for fish and invertebrates. IUCN Vulnerable due to extensive mangrove loss and hunting across its fragmented island range.

children.name: Mycteria ibis
children.description: A striking African stork with pinkish-white plumage, bare red facial skin, and a vivid yellow bill; widespread in sub-Saharan Africa's lakes, rivers, and floodplains. Uses tactile open-and-snap feeding, sweeping its bill through shallow water to detect fish by touch. Nests colonially in large mixed heronries, often with egrets and other wading birds. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Mycteria leucocephala
children.description: One of Asia's most ornate wading birds, with a bold patterning of white, black, and deep pink plumage and an orange-yellow bill. Found across freshwater wetlands of South and Southeast Asia; nests in spectacular colonial rookeries often shared with herons and ibises. A tactile feeder specialising in shallow-water fish. IUCN Least Concern.
